title: Pre-Trained Language Models for Interactive Decision-Making

structured.summary: The paper explores the application of pre-trained language models (LMs) in sequential decision-making tasks using an LID framework, demonstrating improvement in task completion through a combination of LM-based policy initialization and active data gathering (ADG). While the results highlight improved generalization in environments like VirtualHome, the paper lacks in-depth methodological details and broader experimental validation.

strengths.point: Demonstrated task improvement with LID framework using LMs.
strengths.evidence: Task completion rates increased by 43.6% in the VirtualHome environment (Abstract, lines 9-10).

strengths.point: Integration of active data gathering (ADG) for environments without expert data.
strengths.evidence: ADG outperformed the best baseline by 25.1% in environments without pre-collected expert data (Abstract, lines 12-16).

weaknesses.evidence: LMs have been used beyond language processing, as noted in prior work like [5], [23], and [36] (Introduction, lines 88-112).

weaknesses.point: Insufficient methodological detail in ADG procedure.
weaknesses.evidence: Minimal details are given about relabeling trajectories and policy updates (Sections 1, lines 52-60).

weaknesses.point: Weak focus on evaluation metrics beyond task completion rates.
weaknesses.evidence: The experimental results focus predominantly on task completion rates (Abstract, lines 9-10 and 16).

weaknesses.point: Ambiguous interpretation of result contributions from LMs.
weaknesses.evidence: Improvements are claimed but insufficient clarity on specific contributions by inputs or ADG (Abstract, lines 19-23).

weaknesses.point: Limited experimental validation primarily in VirtualHome.
weaknesses.evidence: Results are heavily centered around VirtualHome with no validation in other environments (Methods, lines 41-50).

weaknesses.point: Insufficient baseline comparisons and specific details.
weaknesses.evidence: Abstract does not sufficiently specify baselines, only refers generically (Abstract, lines 16 and 44).

weaknesses.point: Overemphasis on the role of pre-trained weights while excluding other factors.
weaknesses.evidence: Effectiveness attributed mainly to pre-training of weights (Section 1, lines 69-71, 80-82).

weaknesses.point: Clarity issues with redundancy and phrasing.
weaknesses.evidence: Repetition of claims without adding informative details (Introduction, Abstract, and Contributions sections).

weaknesses.point: Reproducibility gaps due to lack of algorithmic details.
weaknesses.evidence: No code or pseudo-code for policy updates or ADG procedures (entire methods section).

weaknesses.point: Contextual misalignment for claims of extending LMs to non-linguistic tasks.
weaknesses.evidence: Claim LMs inherently model goals and plans without deep analytical support (Abstract, lines 22-23).

weaknesses.point: Limited analysis of sequential versus fixed-dimensional inputs.
weaknesses.evidence: Section claims benefits of sequential representation without in-depth analysis on performance differences.

weaknesses.point: No exploration of computational costs associated with using LMs in decision-making tasks.
weaknesses.evidence: The paper does not address the computational demands of integrating LMs into these tasks.

questions.question: How does the proposed ADG mechanism compare to other self-supervised learning approaches?
questions.motivation: Understanding how ADG stands in comparison to other methodologies provides context on its effectiveness.
